elements.sent-2411-001: Some qualitative properties of radially symmetric solutions to the non-homogeneous heat equation with critical density and weighted source \begin{equation}|x|^{-2}\partial_tu=\Delta u+|x|^{\sigma}u^p, \quad (x,t)\in\real^N\times(0,T),\end{equation} are obtained, in the range of exponents $p>1$, $\sigma\ge-2$.
elements.sent-2411-002: More precisely, we establish conditions fulfilled by the initial data in order for the solutions to either blow-up in finite time or decay to zero as $t\to\infty$ and, in the latter case, we also deduce decay rates and large time behavior.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the limiting case $\sigma=-2$ we prove the existence of non-trivial, non-negative solutions, in stark contrast to the homogeneous case.
elements.sent-2411-004: A transformation to a generalized Fisher-KPP equation is derived and employed in order to deduce these properties.
elements.sent-2411-005: The goal of this short note is to establish some properties of solutions to the following non-homogeneous heat equation with a weighted source \begin{equation}\label{eq1} |x|^{-2}\partial_tu=\Delta u+|x|^{\sigma}u^p, \quad (x,t)\in\real^N\times(0,T), \quad T\in(0,\infty],\end{equation} in the range of exponents $\sigma\ge-2$, $p>1$.
elements.sent-2411-006: More precisely, we consider radially symmetric solutions to \eqref{eq1 and we aim at establishing conditions on the exponents $p$, $\sigma$ and on the (radially symmetric) initial condition \begin{equation}\label{ic} u(x,0)=u_0(|x|)\in C(\real^N)\cap L^{\infty}(\real^N), \quad u_0\geq0, \quad u_0\not\equiv0,\end{equation} implying either finite time blow-up of solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq1 or global existence and decay as $t\to\infty$, and in the latter case giving a decay rate and (under suitable conditions) the large time behavior as $t\to\infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: In order to ease the notation, we denote the radial variable by $r=|x|$ and we recall here for the readers' convenience that, in this variable, Eq. \eqref{eq1 writes \begin{equation}\label{eq2} r^{-2}u_t=u_{rr}+\frac{N-1}{r}u_r+r^{\sigma}u^p,\end{equation} where, as usual, the subscripts indicate derivatives.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Eq. \eqref{eq1 features a competition between a non-homogeneous heat equation with a weight (usually referred as density function by physical reasons) $\varrho(x)=|x|^{-2}$ and a source term also weighted with a power $|x|^{\sigma}$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Equations in the more general form \begin{equation}\label{nhpme} \varrho(x)u_t=\Delta u^m+q(x)u^p, \quad m\geq1, \quad p>1,\end{equation} for suitable functions $\varrho(x)$ (usually called density function) and $q(x)$ have been proposed in several models in radial transport in confined plasma \cite{KR81, KR82 , in the theory of combustion with a power-law temperature depending on the source \cite{KKMS80, KK04 , or (without the reaction term) in a kinetic model that describes the evolution of the probability density of the number of firms in a society \cite{T16 , just to give some examples.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Such models justified a development of the mathematical theory of equations in the form \eqref{nhpme , with or without a reaction term, and such equations are usually referred in literature under the name of non-homogeneous heat equation (if $m=1$) or non-homogeneous porous medium equation (if $m>1$).}
elements.sent-2411-011: It has been thus noticed that densities $\varrho(x)$ either being exactly equal to $|x|^{-2}$ or behaving as $|x|^{-2}$ as $|x|\to\infty$ are critical for the dynamic properties of \eqref{nhpme .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, restricting ourselves to our case of interest $m=1$, a number of works have addressed the mathematical analysis of solutions to Eq. \eqref{nhpme , but the case $\varrho(x)\sim |x|^{-2}$ has been avoided therein, see for example \cite{dPRS13, LX14, ZW08 , where densities behaving like $|x|^{-\sigma}$ are considered for either $\sigma\in(0,2)$ or $\sigma>2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: A similar criticality of $\varrho(x)=|x|^{-2}$ has been observed also in the porous medium case $m>1$ in \cite{IS14, KRV10, KKMS80, KK04, MT07, MP20, MP21, MP21b, RV06 , to quote but a few references dealing with the mathematical study of \eqref{nhpme .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Regarding the equation \eqref{nhpme with $\varrho(x)=|x|^{-2}$, the authors established well-posedness and large time behavior of solutions to \eqref{nhpme with $m=1$ and $q(x)=0$ in \cite{IS13 (generalizing then the technique also to the non-homogeneous porous medium equation without source term in \cite{IS14 ), these properties being deduced by exploiting a transformation mapping its radially symmetric solutions to those of a standard heat equation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Afterwards, Toscani described in \cite{T16 a kinetic model leading to a rather similar equation and employing an alternative transformation in its study.}
elements.sent-2411-016: More recently, the authors considered in \cite{IS23 a general class of equations with two weights, namely \begin{equation}\label{eq1.gen} |x|^{\sigma_1}\partial_tu=\Delta u^m+|x|^{\sigma_2}u^p\end{equation} and established a number of qualitative properties of their solutions (such as Fujita-type exponents, second critical exponent limiting between finite time blow-up and global existence, classification of self-similar solutions) by introducing and then exploiting a number of transformations, at the level of radially symmetric solutions, mapping solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq1.gen to solutions to some other reaction-diffusion equations whose properties were well-established prior to this research.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, Eq. \eqref{eq1 (which is actually a limiting case for the ranges considered in \cite{IS23 ) has been left out of the study in \cite{IS23 due to the fact that the main bunch of transformations therein were not applicable to it.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The present work is thus devoted to fill in this gap by establishing some qualitative properties realted to the evolution of radially symmetric solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq1 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This is done by means of a transformation detailed in Section \ref{sec.transf mapping such solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq1 to solutions to a generalized Fisher-KPP type equation, which allows us to translate features of the latter equation to the former one by undoing the transformation.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: With the aid of it, we are able to give conditions on $p>1$ and on the radially symmetric initial condition $u_0$ such that the solution to the Cauchy problem \eqref{eq1 -\eqref{ic either blows up in a finite time $T\in(0,\infty)$ or is global in time and decays to zero as $t\to\infty$; in the latter, we also give the large time behavior of such solutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In order to fix the notation employed throughout this short note, let us introduce here two critical exponents who will play a significant role in the forthcoming study, \begin{equation}\label{crit.exp} p_c(\sigma):=\frac{N+\sigma}{N-2}, \qquad p_s(\sigma):=\frac{N+2\sigma+2}{N-2}, \quad N\geq3,\end{equation} the second being usually referred to as the Sobolev critical exponent in the context of the reaction-diffusion equation \begin{equation}\partial_tu=\Delta u+|x|^{\sigma}u^p,\end{equation} see for example \cite{FT00, MS21 , both of them being equal by convention to $+\infty$ in dimension $N\in\{1,2\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In particular, the analysis in Sections \ref{sec.bu and \ref{subsec.ltb shows that $p_c(\sigma)$ is the Fujita-type exponent to Eq. \eqref{eq1 in dimension $N\geq3$, while $p_s(\sigma)$ appears in relation to the behavior of a new explicit solution being a separatrix between global existence and blow-up, see Section \ref{subsec.sep .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Before passing to the precise statements and proofs, we stress that we do not aim at constructing a functional-analytic theory of Eq. \eqref{eq1 in this note.}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, in the particular framework of radial symmetry, the well-posedness for Eq. \eqref{eq2 follows directly from the well-posedness property of the resulting Fisher-KPP type equation by undoing the transformation in Section \ref{sec.transf .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: We refrain here from entering the more detailed discussion of a notion of weak solution and the well-posedness, regularity and other properties of Eq. \eqref{eq1 without the assumption of radial symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The following transformation, which is the main tool in our analysis, has been announced (but not employed in any form) in \cite{IS23 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: We describe it below in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-028: Let $u$ be a radially symmetric solution to Eq. \eqref{eq1 , that is, a solution to Eq. \eqref{eq2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In a first step, set \begin{equation}\label{transf1} w(y,t)=r^{\frac{\sigma+2}{p-1}}u(r,t), \quad y=\ln\,r, \quad r\in(0,\infty).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-030: After performing straightforward calculations, we deduce that $w(y,t)$ solves the equation \begin{equation}\label{interm1} w_t=w_{yy}+Kw_y-K_0w+w^p,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{interm2} K_0:=\frac{\sigma+2}{p-1}\left[N-2-\frac{\sigma+2}{p-1}\right], \quad K:=N-2-\frac{2(\sigma+2)}{p-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: Observe that, for $N\geq3$, the previous constants can be written in terms of the critical exponents \eqref{crit.exp as \begin{equation}K_0=\frac{(\sigma+2)(N-2)}{(p-1)^2}(p-p_c(\sigma)), \quad K=\frac{N-2}{p-1}(p-p_s(\sigma)).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In a second step, we introduce the function $\Psi$ as follows: \begin{equation}\label{transf2} \Psi(z,t)=w(y,t), \quad z=y+Kt,\end{equation} with $K$ defined in \eqref{interm2 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Then the function $\Psi$ solves the following one-dimensional generalized Fisher-KPP type equation \begin{equation}\label{Fisher} \Psi_t=\Psi_{zz}-K_0\Psi+\Psi^p.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-034: We may thus compose the transformations \eqref{transf1 and \eqref{transf2 to map \eqref{eq2 into \eqref{Fisher .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Let us also notice here that the initial conditions for the two equations are mapped in the following way \begin{equation}\label{transf.ic} u_0(r)=r^{-(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}w_0(r)=e^{-(\sigma+2)z/(p-1)}\Psi_0(z),\end{equation} a fact that is useful when dealing with Cauchy problems.
elements.sent-2411-036: Since the well posedness of \eqref{Fisher is a standard property in the sense of classical solutions (for the uniqueness and comparison principle, even in more general cases, see for example \cite{DK12, DJ23 ), and the transformations \eqref{transf1 and \eqref{transf2 preserve the order between two solutions at a fixed point $(r,t)$, we obtain local existence and uniqueness for \eqref{eq2 by undoing the previous transformations (with solutions that remain classical except, in some cases, at $r=0$).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The properties of solutions to \eqref{Fisher needed in the forthcoming analysis have been deduced by the authors (in a framework of a more general study) in \cite{IS24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: We are thus in a position to translate these features into properties of solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq2 by undoing the previous changes of variable \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Then any non-trivial, non-negative radially symmetric solution to Eq. \eqref{eq1 blows up in finite time.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Notice that any of the conditions $N\in\{1,2\}$ or $N\geq3$ and $1<p<p_c(\sigma)$ implies $K_0<0$, where $K_0$ is defined in \eqref{interm2 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Thus, \eqref{Fisher features in this case a sum of reaction terms.}
elements.sent-2411-043: It is rather obvious then that any solution to \eqref{Fisher blows up in finite time, but for the sake of completeness we give some details.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Let $\Psi$ be a non-negative, non-trivial solution to \eqref{Fisher .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Then, in particular $\Psi$ is a supersolution to the linear equation \begin{equation}\label{interm3} \Psi_t=\Psi_{zz}+|K_0|\psi.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-046: Let $\Psi$ be the solution to \eqref{interm3 with initial condition $\Psi(z,0)=\Psi(z,0)$ and introduce \begin{equation}\Phi(z,t)=e^{-|K_0|t}\Psi(z,t).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Then $\Phi(z,0)=\Psi(z,0)$ and $\Phi$ is a solution to the classical one-dimensional heat equation.
elements.sent-2411-048: It then follows from standard results related to the heat equation that \begin{equation}\Psi(z,t)=e^{|K_0|t}\Phi(z,t)\end{equation} has at least an exponential time growth on compact subsets.
elements.sent-2411-049: By comparison, the same holds true for $\Psi$.
elements.sent-2411-050: But, on the other hand, $\Psi$ is also a supersolution to \begin{equation}\label{interm4} \Psi_t=\Psi_{zz}+\Psi^p,\end{equation} and, since it has an exponential time growth, one can readily see that the energy \begin{equation}E[\Psi](t)=\frac{1}{2}\int_{\real}|\nabla\Psi(z,t)|^2\,dz-\frac{1}{p+1}\int_{\real}\Psi^{p+1}(z,t)\,dz\end{equation} becomes negative for some $t_0>0$ sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-051: Considering this $t_0$ as initial time for comparison, finite time blow-up of $\Psi$ is then ensured by \cite[Theorem 17.6]{QS and the comparison principle.}
elements.sent-2411-052: We end the proof by undoing the transformations \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 .}}
elements.section-2411-004: Transition from decay to blow-up for $p>p_c(\sigma)$
elements.sent-2411-053: In the range $p>p_c(\sigma)$, we observe that $K_0>0$ and thus \eqref{Fisher is a generalized Fisher-KPP type equation, featuring a competition between a (linear) absorption term and a source term.}
elements.sent-2411-054: As established in \cite{IS24 , this fact leads to more interesting situations than in the previous section.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Throughout this section, we fix $N\geq3$ and $p>p_c(\sigma)$, the case $p=p_c(\sigma)$ being considered in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-056: Let us first remark that, for $p>p_c(\sigma)$, Eq. \eqref{eq1 admits a stationary solution (with a singularity at $x=0$), \begin{equation}\label{stat.sol} S(r):=K_0^{1/(p-1)}r^{-(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this section we establish the decay rate as $t\to\infty$ and the large time behavior of solutions whose initial condition lies below the singular stationary solution \eqref{stat.sol .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Furthermore, we establish at least a class of solutions that remain bounded globally in time, completing the characterization of $p_c(\sigma)$ as the Fujita-type exponent for Eq. \eqref{eq1 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Let $u_0$ be an initial condition as in \eqref{ic such that $u_0(r)<S(r)$ for any $r\in(0,\infty)$.. Then.}
elements.sent-2411-061: There exists $C=C(p,\sigma,u_0)>0$ such that the solution $u$ to Eq. \eqref{eq1 with initial condition $u_0$ satisfies \begin{equation}\label{decay} u(r,t)\leq Cr^{-(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}e^{-K_0t}, \quad (r,t)\in(0,\infty)\times(0,\infty).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-062: If, furthermore, the following weighted integral is finite \begin{equation}\label{int.weight} M(u_0):=\int_0^{\infty}r^{(\sigma+2)/(p-1)-1}u_0(r)\,dr<\infty,\end{equation} then the large time behavior of $u$ as $t\to\infty$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{asympt.beh} \lim\limits_{t\to\infty}t^{1/2}\left[e^{K_0t}r^{(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}u(r,t)-G(r+Kt,t)\right]=0,\end{equation} with uniform convergence over compact sets in $(0,\infty)$, where $K_0$, $K$ are defined in \eqref{interm2 and \begin{equation}G(\zeta,t)=\frac{M(u_0)}{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-\zeta^2/4t},\end{equation} is the Gaussian kernel.}
elements.sent-2411-063: If, moreover, ${\rm supp}\,u_0\subset\real^N\setminus\{0\}$ is a compact set, then $u(0,t)=0$ for any $t\in(0,\infty)$ and in particular $u(t)\in L^{\infty (\real^N)$ for any $t>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Let $u_0$ be as in the statement.
elements.sent-2411-065: We infer from \eqref{transf.ic that, by applying the transformation \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 , the radially symmetric solution to Eq. \eqref{eq1 with initial condition $u_0$ is mapped into the solution to \eqref{Fisher with an initial condition $\Psi_0$ satisfying \begin{equation}\label{interm5} \|\Psi_0\|_{\infty}<K_0^{1/(p-1)}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We derive from \eqref{interm5 and \cite[Theorem 1]{IS24 that the solution $\Psi$ to \eqref{Fisher with initial condition $\Psi_0$ is global, decays to zero as $t\to\infty$ and, more precisely, there is $C>0$ (depending on $\|\Psi_0\|_{\infty}$) such that \begin{equation}\Psi(z,t)\leq Ce^{-K_0t}, \quad (z,t)\in\real\times(0,\infty).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The decay estimate \eqref{decay follows then from the previous inequality by undoing \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: We infer from \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 that this condition entails \begin{equation}\int_{\real}\Psi_0(z)\,dz=\int_{\real}w_0(y)\,dy=\int_0^{\infty}\frac{w_0(r)}{r}\,dr=\int_0^{\infty}r^{(\sigma+2)/(p-1)-1}u_0(r)\,dr<\infty,\end{equation} thus $\Psi_0\in L^1(\real)$ and $M(u_0)=\|\Psi_0\|_1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We then deduce the large time behavior \eqref{asympt.beh (with uniform convergence) from \eqref{interm6 by undoing the transformation \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Finally, assume that the initial condition $u_0$ is compactly supported in a set contained in $\real^N\setminus\{0\}$, or, in terms of radial variables, in a compact interval of $(0,\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-073: We then deduce from \eqref{transf.ic that the initial condition $\Psi_0$ to \eqref{Fisher is compactly supported in $(-\infty,\infty)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $\tilde{\Psi}$ be the solution to the Cauchy problem \begin{equation}\label{interm9} \begin{split} \tilde{\Psi}_{t}-\tilde{\Psi}_{zz}=0, \quad (z,t)\in\real\times(0,\infty), \tilde{\Psi}(z,0)=\Psi_0(z), \quad z\in\real. \end{split}\end{equation} Since $\|\Psi_0\|_{\infty <K_0^{1/(p-1)}$, it readily follows by the comparison principle that \begin{equation}\label{interm10} \|\tilde{\Psi}(t)\|_{\infty}<K_0^{1/(p-1)}, \quad t\in(0,\infty).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Indeed, taking into account \eqref{interm9 and \eqref{interm10 , we compute \begin{equation}\tilde{\Psi}_t(z,t)-\tilde{\Psi}_{zz}(z,t)+K_0\tilde{\Psi}(z,t)-\tilde{\Psi}^p(z,t)=\tilde{\Psi}(z,t)(K_0-\tilde{\Psi}^{p-1}(z,t))>0,\end{equation} for any $(z,t)\in\real\times(0,\infty)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: It thus follows from the comparison principle applied to \eqref{Fisher that $\Psi(z,t)\leq\tilde{\Psi (z,t)$ for any $(z,t)\in\real\times(0,\infty)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Moreover, by standard results for the heat equation, there is a mapping $t\mapsto z(t)$ and a positive constant $C(t)$ such that we have \begin{equation}\Psi(z,t)\leq\tilde{\Psi}(z,t)\leq C(t)e^{-z^2/4t}, \quad t>0, \quad z\in(-\infty,z(t)),\end{equation} whence, by undoing the transformations \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 , we obtain that $u(0,t)=0$ for any $t>0$ and that the solution $u$ remains bounded in a neighborhood of $r=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: This fact, together with \eqref{decay , ensures that $u(t)\in L^{\infty (\real^N)$ for any $t\in(0,\infty)$, completing the proof.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The previous statement does not take into account the behavior of the function $t\mapsto u(r,t)$ at $r=0$ when we start with data such that $u_0(0)>0$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Indeed, a priori, the result of Theorem \ref{th.decay stays true even when $u(r,t)$ might blow-up in the sense of developing a vertical asymptote at $r=0$ with a weaker singularity than $r^{-(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}$, while remaining bounded (and decaying in time as in the statement) at any point $x\in\real^N$ with $r=|x|>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: This property is related, by the transformation \eqref{transf1 -\eqref{transf2 , with the question of whether, if $\Psi$ is a solution to \eqref{Fisher such that \begin{equation}\label{interm8} \Psi_0(z)\sim Ce^{(\sigma+2)z/(p-1)}, \quad {\rm as} \ z\to-\infty,\end{equation} then its tail is preserved at later times, that is, \begin{equation}\Psi(z,t)\leq\overline{C}e^{(\sigma+2)z/(p-1)}, \quad z\in(-\infty,z(t)), \quad t>0.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Up to our knowledge, there is no reference proving such a result for the precise decay \eqref{interm8 , but it is established in \cite{He99 that tails of the form $\Psi(z)\sim A|z|^{-\alpha}$ as $|z|\to\infty$ are preserved during the evolution for solutions to the reaction-diffusion equation \begin{equation}\Psi_t=\Psi_{zz}+\Psi^p,\end{equation} and by comparison, the upper bound for the tail remains in force for \eqref{Fisher .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: It is very likely (as suggested by the author of \cite{He99 , who claims that the techniques therein apply for more general decays) that the same property holds true for initial conditions decaying with a precise exponential tail as $z\to-\infty$ (such as, for example, \eqref{interm8 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: However, due to the very technical and lengthy character of the proofs in \cite{He99 , we refrain from developing this subject in the present note.}
elements.subsection-2411-002: A new explicit solution as a separatrix between decay and blow-up
elements.sent-2411-086: In this section, we introduce a new explicit radially symmetric solution to Eq. \eqref{eq1 and show that it is a separatrix between decay and blow-up, in some conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-087: As we shall see, the Sobolev critical exponent $p_s(\sigma)$ defined in \eqref{crit.exp plays a significant role.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We start from the following stationary solution to \eqref{Fisher obtained in \cite[Section 2.1]{IS24 \begin{equation}\label{stat.fisher} \overline{\Psi}(z)=\left[\frac{K_0(p+1)}{2}\right]^{1/(p-1)}\left[1-\tanh^2\left(\frac{(p-1)\sqrt{K_0}}{2}z\right)\right]^{1/(p-1)},\end{equation} which, by undoing first the transformation \eqref{transf2 leads to \begin{equation}W(y,t)=\left[\frac{K_0(p+1)}{2}\right]^{1/(p-1)}\left[1-\tanh^2\left(\frac{(p-1)\sqrt{K_0}}{2}(y+Kt)\right)\right]^{1/(p-1)}\end{equation} and finally, by undoing also \eqref{transf1 , gives the following explicit radially symmetric solution to Eq. \eqref{eq1 \begin{equation}\label{expl.sol} U(r,t)=\left[\frac{K_0(p+1)}{2}r^{-(\sigma+2)}\right]^{1/(p-1)}\left[1-\tanh^2\left(\frac{(p-1)\sqrt{K_0}}{2}(\ln\,r+Kt)\right)\right]^{1/(p-1)}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Observe that $U$ is an eternal solution to Eq. \eqref{eq1 , in the sense that it is well defined for $t\in(-\infty,\infty)$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: By employing the identity \begin{equation}1-\tanh^2(\theta)=\frac{4}{2+e^{2\theta}+e^{-2\theta}}, \quad \theta=\frac{(p-1)\sqrt{K_0}}{2}(\ln\,r+Kt)\end{equation} we obtain from \eqref{expl.sol that, for any $t>0$, \begin{equation}\label{beh.stat} \begin{split} U(r,t)\sim[2K_0(p+1)]^{1/(p-1)}r^{\sqrt{K_0}-(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}e^{\sqrt{K_0}Kt}, \quad {\rm as} \ r\to0, U(r,t)\sim[2K_0(p+1)]^{1/(p-1)}r^{-\sqrt{K_0}-(\sigma+2)/(p-1)}e^{-\sqrt{K_0}Kt}, \quad {\rm as} \ r\to\infty, \end{split}\end{equation} where, as usual, the equivalence symbol $\sim$ means that the quotient between the two sides tends to one in the specified limit, and we recall that $K$ and $K_0$ are defined in \eqref{interm2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: We observe that $U$ always decays as $r\to\infty$, but the local behavior at $r=0$ and the variation with respect to time are more interesting.
elements.sent-2411-092: Indeed, we notice on the one hand that \begin{equation}K_0-\left(\frac{\sigma+2}{p-1}\right)^2=\frac{(\sigma+2)(N-2)}{(p-1)^2}[p-p_s(\sigma)],\end{equation} thus $p=p_s(\sigma)$ is critical with respect to the behavior at $r=0$.
elements.sent-2411-093: On the other hand, studying the variation with respect to time of the expression \begin{equation}h(t):=\frac{4}{2+e^{(p-1)\sqrt{K_0}(\ln\,r+Kt)}+e^{-(p-1)\sqrt{K_0}(\ln\,r+Kt)}},\end{equation} we observe that it behaves in different ways in dynamic inner sets $\{0<r<e^{-Kt}\}$ and in dynamic outer sets $\{r>e^{-Kt}\}$, for $t>0$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Gathering the previous analysis, we have: $\bullet$ if $p\in(p_c(\sigma),p_s(\sigma))$, then $U$ is a singular solution, presenting a vertical asymptote at $r=0$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Moreover, according to \eqref{beh.stat and the negativity of $K$ in this range, the solution $U$ decays with respect to time in the inner set $\mathcal{I}$ and increases with respect to time in the outer set $\mathcal{O}$ defined below: \begin{equation}\label{inner.outer} \mathcal{I}:=\{(r,t)\in(0,\infty)^2: 0<r<e^{-Kt}\}, \quad \mathcal{O}:=\{(r,t)\in(0,\infty)^2: e^{-Kt}<r\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Moreover, according to \eqref{beh.stat and the positivity of $K$ in this range, the solution $U$ increases with respect to time in the inner set $\mathcal{I}$ and decreases with respect to time in the outer set $\mathcal{O}$ introduced in \eqref{inner.outer .}}
elements.sent-2411-097: In order to better illustrate the previous behavior in inner and outer sets, for the readers' convenience we plot the solution $U$ at different times in Figure \ref{fig1 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \subfigure[$p<p_s(\sigma)$]{\includegraphics[width=7.5cm,height=6cm]{explicita22.pdf}} \subfigure[$p>p_s(\sigma)$]{\includegraphics[width=7.5cm,height=6cm]{explicita12.pdf}} \end{center} \caption{The solution $U$ plotted at different times. elements.sent-2411-105: These two critical cases are connected by the common fact that, either if $\sigma>-2$ and $p=p_c(\sigma)$ or if $\sigma=-2$ and $p>1$ arbitrary, we have $K_0=0$ and thus \eqref{Fisher becomes the standard reaction-diffusion equation \begin{equation}\label{eq.rd} \Psi_t=\Psi_{zz}+\Psi^{p},\end{equation} which is well understood nowadays (see, for example, the monograph \cite{QS and references therein).}}

elements.sent-2411-132: It thus appears that the presence of the critical density function $|x|^{-2}$ in front of $u_t$ is the decisive feature that guarantees the existence of positive solutions.

elements.sent-2411-134: As a particular case of part (b), we also infer that, if there is $\delta>0$ such that $u_0(r)=0$ for $r\in(0,\delta)$, then $u(0,t)=0$ for any $t\in(0,T(u_0))$, similarly to what we proved for $\sigma>-2$ and $p>p_c(\sigma)$ in Theorem \ref{th.decay .}

elements.sent-2411-136: In this paper we have studied a number of properties of solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq1 , which presents as main feature a critical density function $|x|^{-2}$ pondering the time derivative.}
elements.sent-2411-137: On the one hand, we have shown that the critical exponent $p_c(\sigma)$ plays the role of a Fujita-type exponent, limiting between the range of blow-up for any non-trivial solution and possible existence of global solutions to the equation.
elements.sent-2411-138: On the other hand, in the range $p>p_c(\sigma)$ we have introduced a new explicit solution which serves as a separatrix between solutions presenting finite time blow-up and solutions decaying to zero as $t\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Moreover, we provide the large time behavior of such solutions, towards a profile related to the Gaussian kernel.
elements.sent-2411-140: Finally, we show local existence of solutions for $\sigma=-2$, in contrast with the equation with a singular potential alone which is known for non-existence of any solution.
elements.sent-2411-141: The fundamental tool employed in all this proofs is a new transformation mapping solutions to Eq. \eqref{eq1 into solutions to a generalized Fisher-KPP equation which is also itself of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-142: We believe that our work contributes to improve the understanding of equations involving singular potentials and densities.. Acknowledgements R.
elements.sent-2411-143: Data availability Our manuscript has no associated data.
elements.sent-2411-144: Competing interest The authors declare that there is no competing interest.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}
